w:r.w:t: UI/UX DESIGN SPECIFICATION & API DESIGN DOCUMENT
Design System - Screen Specs - Endpoint Catalog - Webhooks - Error Codes - Blueprint Closure
Vol. 1 Personas & Value Streams; Vol. 2 Resource Ownership Map; Vol. 3 React/Component Library Decision & API Conventions; Vol. 5 Webhook Platform; Vol. 6 Auth Model

This is the ninth and final volume of the Enterprise Architecture Blueprint. It has two parts. Part A is the UI/UX Design Specification: the design system, information architecture, and screen-level specifications for the six value streams defined in Vol. 1 Section 7, built on the React/component-library decision from Vol. 3 Section 2.3. Part B is the API Design Document: the full endpoint catalog expanding Vol. 2 Section 11's resource-ownership map, request/response examples, the webhook payload catalog expanding Vol. 5 Section 7.1, the error code reference formalizing Vol. 3 Section 4.3, and versioning policy. Section 14 closes the entire blueprint with a full nine-volume traceability index.
1.2 Why UI/UX and API Design Share One Volume

The UI is explicitly a client of the same API every third-party integrator uses (Vol. 2 Principle, Vol. 3 Section 5) - there is no separate internal API. Documenting them together keeps that principle enforced by construction: every screen specification in Part A cites the exact endpoint(s) in Part B it calls, so the two documents cannot drift apart the way a UI spec and an API spec maintained separately often do.
Navy (#1F3864) for brand/headers, Accent Blue (#2E75B6) for interactive elements
Matches this blueprint's own document styling for internal consistency
Used consistently for invoice/bill status badges (Vol.2 Section 8.1 state machine colors)
System font stack for performance; a distinct monospace for financial figures to aid column alignment
Financial amounts always right-aligned, tabular-figure font variant
Consistent density across dense financial tables and lighter marketing-style portal pages
Keeps modal/dropdown hierarchy visually unambiguous in dense screens like the invoice editor

Built on the Vol. 3 Section 2.3 decision (React + a headless UI kit such as Radix primitives). Core components: data table (with sort/filter/pagination matching Vol. 3 Section 5's cursor-pagination convention), form inputs with inline validation (surfacing the namespaced error codes from Section 11), status badge, money input (locale-aware, Vol. 1 BC-12), date range picker, modal/drawer, toast/notification, and the recurring approval-workflow widget (Vol. 1 BC-16).
Every screen spec (Section 4) ships with a component inventory referencing this section's library - no ad hoc one-off components without an explicit exception noted.
Design tokens (Section 2.1) are implemented as a shared theme file consumed by both the web app and portal app (Vol.3 Section 3.2 /libs/shared/ui-components), never hand-copied per screen.
Accessibility annotations (Section 5) are part of the handoff artifact, not a separate post-hoc audit.

Top-level navigation is organized by bounded context (Vol. 2 Section 3.2), matching how permissions are granted (Vol. 6 Section 3.3) so a user's nav only ever shows sections they can act on - there is no navigation item that leads to a permission-denied screen.
All roles (scoped to their own data visibility)
All roles (report set varies by role)

A deliberately minimal, separate navigation (Vol. 6 Section 3.1 portal auth) - customers see Invoices, Payments, and Messages; vendors see Purchase Orders, Bills, and Messages. Neither portal exposes any internal-staff navigation item, consistent with the portal's restricted API scope (Vol. 2 Section 11).

Each value stream from Vol. 1 Section 7 gets its key screens specified here at a wireframe-description level of detail (not pixel-perfect mockups, which belong to a dedicated design tool) - enough for engineering to build against and for the API cross-references (Part B) to be concrete.
Data table: customer, number, status badge, amount, due date; filter by status; bulk actions (send reminder)
Customer selector, line-item table with item/qty/price/tax, totals summary, save-as-draft vs send actions
Amount, method, date, reference number; partial-payment support with running balance display
Read-only invoice detail, Pay Now button (Vol.5 Section 4.3 payment link), comment thread
Vendor selector, line items, expected delivery date, approval-status indicator (Vol.1 BC-03)
Side-by-side PO vs Receipt vs Bill comparison, variance highlighting (Vol.2 Section 9.3), approve/flag actions
Upload/submit bill against a received PO, status tracker
Hierarchical account tree, add/edit account, system-defined accounts locked from deletion (Vol.4 Section 4.1)
Multi-line debit/credit grid with running balance-check indicator (must net to zero before save)
Checklist UI walking through reconciliation status, outstanding approvals, then period lock confirmation (Vol.1 VS-06)
P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow with period comparison selector
Desktop-first for the core web app (dense financial tables genuinely need width); portal app is mobile-first (Vol.1 PER-09/PER-10 are more likely on mobile)
WCAG 2.1 AA across both web app and portal - color contrast, keyboard navigation, screen-reader labeling on every form input and status badge
Every action reachable via keyboard alone, critical for the dense data-entry screens (invoice/bill editors, Section 4)
Status is never color-only (Vol.2 Section 8.1 state machine) - every status badge pairs color with a text label and, where relevant, an icon
Every namespaced error code (Section 11) maps to a human-readable, actionable message - never surfacing a raw error code to an end user
6. API Conventions (Recap & Expansion of Vol. 3 Section 5)
URL-path versioned (Vol.3 Section 5); this document specifies v1 fully
Bearer JWT (Vol.6 Section 3.2); portal endpoints use a separate portal-scoped token
Idempotency-Key header required on all financial-mutating POSTs (Vol.3 Section 5, Vol.5 Principle I4)
application/json exclusively; file uploads (attachments, Vol.1 BC-14) use multipart/form-data on dedicated endpoints
Expands Vol. 2 Section 11's resource-ownership table into concrete methods and paths. This is not exhaustive of every field but fixes the full surface area every module must implement.
Authenticate, returns access + refresh token (Vol.6 Section 3.1)
Exchange refresh token for a new access token
List / create custom roles (Vol.6 Section 4.2 ceiling rule enforced server-side)
Create a manual journal entry (Vol.4 Section 4.1 chk_one_sided enforced)
Fetch a journal entry with its lines
Convert quote to sales order (Vol.1 VS-01)
Record a payment against an invoice
Void an invoice (append-only reversal, Vol.4 Section 2 D2/D6)
Match a transaction to an invoice/bill payment
Create a payment link for an invoice (Vol.5 Section 4.3)
Generate P&L for a date range
Generate balance sheet as of a date
Run a saved custom report (Vol.1 BC-13)
invoiceId, invoiceNumber, customerId, totalAmount, currency, sentAt
Attempted to void/edit an invoice that is already fully paid
Invoice/sales order line item exceeds available inventory (Vol.2 Section 9.1)
Attempted to post to a locked fiscal period (Vol.1 VS-06)
Journal entry debits and credits do not match (Vol.4 Section 4.1 chk_one_sided)
3-way match found a quantity or price variance requiring manual review (Vol.2 Section 9.3)
Action requires MFA verification (Vol.6 Section 3.1) not yet completed in this session
Idempotency-Key reused with a different request body
Tenant/API-key rate limit exceeded (Vol.3 Section 7.1, Vol.5 Section 7.2)
A new major version (/v2) is introduced only for breaking changes; additive fields and new optional parameters never require a version bump (Vol.3 Section 5).
A deprecated version is supported for a minimum of 12 months after its successor ships, with deprecation notices returned via a response header on every call.

Webhook payload schemas (Section 9) follow the same additive-only rule as internal domain events (Vol.3 Section 4.2 CS-06); a breaking payload change ships as a new event name, not a mutated existing one, so existing integrator subscriptions never silently break.

A public developer portal (documentation, API key management, webhook subscription management, Vol.5 Section 7.1) is a Release 2/3 capability. At Release 1, API documentation is published as a static OpenAPI-generated reference site, and API keys are issued manually by the platform team on request - sufficient for early integrator partners without building a self-service portal before there is integrator demand to justify it.
13. Full Blueprint Closure: Nine-Volume Traceability Index

This is the capstone index for the entire Enterprise Architecture Blueprint. It maps every volume to what it produced and what still depends on it, so the blueprint can be read as a single coherent system rather than nine separate documents.
21 capability domains (BC-01...BC-21), 12 personas, 6 value streams, module scope catalog
16 bounded contexts (CTX-01...CTX-16), C4 model, module dependency graph, event catalog
Stack decisions (NestJS/React/Postgres/Kafka/Redis/OpenSearch), folder structure, coding standards, logging/caching/queueing, multi-tenancy strategy
ER diagrams, physical schema (DDL), RLS implementation, indexing/partitioning, migration & retention strategy
Banking/payment/tax/e-commerce integration design, resilience patterns, public webhook platform
Threat model, IAM/RBAC, encryption/secrets, defense-in-depth, audit trail, compliance mapping
Cloud/managed-service mapping, deployment topology, CI/CD, scaling, observability, DR
Test pyramid, security/performance/resilience test plans, UAT acceptance criteria, release gates
Design system, screen specs, full endpoint catalog, webhook payloads, error codes
14. Blueprint Completion Summary & Recommended Next Steps

Nine volumes now define, at enterprise depth, everything a Zoho Books-class platform needs before development begins: what the business does and for whom (Vol.1), how that decomposes into software boundaries (Vol.2), what it is built with and how it is coded (Vol.3), how data is modeled and protected in the database (Vol.4), how it talks to the outside financial world (Vol.5), how it is secured (Vol.6), how it runs in production (Vol.7), how it is proven correct before every release (Vol.8), and what it looks like and how it is called (Vol.9).

Circulate all nine volumes for stakeholder sign-off, prioritizing the open questions flagged at the end of each volume (target market countries, cloud provider confirmation, QA staffing, SLA commitments) since several later decisions (Vol.5, Vol.7) are contingent on those answers.

Stand up the monorepo (Vol.3 Section 3) and base infrastructure (Vol.7 Section 6 Terraform modules) as the first engineering sprint, before any feature module work begins, so every subsequent module is built against the correct scaffolding from day one.

Sequence Release 1 (MVP) engineering work by the module priority already fixed in Vol.1 Section 6, respecting the layered dependency order in Vol.2 Section 7 (build Identity & Tenant and Ledger first, since every other module depends on them).

Treat this blueprint as a living set of documents: when a real implementation decision deviates from what is written here, update the relevant volume rather than letting the documentation and the system silently drift apart - the whole value of this exercise depends on that discipline.

This concludes the nine-volume Enterprise Architecture Blueprint. Thank you for the opportunity to build this out at the depth you asked for - a genuine engineering foundation rather than a template SRS.
